selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":31,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"delivery driver","city_of_living":"Lecce","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"master's degree","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":1,"migration_status":"native-born Italian","psychological_feature":"creative","religious_beliefs":"Hinduism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly one hour daily on social media","hobbies":["travel","running"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":50,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":86,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":89,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":7,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":12,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

INPUT:
mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":31,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"delivery driver","city_of_living":"Lecce","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"master's degree","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":1,"migration_status":"native-born Italian","psychological_feature":"creative","religious_beliefs":"Hinduism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly one hour daily on social media","hobbies":["travel","running"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":50,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":86,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":89,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":7,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":12,"level":"low"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As someone who spends just an hour a day scrolling through platforms like Facebook, I've found that the constant barrage of misinformation can be draining. My creative nature keeps me somewhat detached from the emotional rollercoaster many people get into when they encounter fake news; it's more about seeing patterns or creating my own narratives than getting worked up over it. Having grown up with parents educated to masters and upper secondary levels, I've learned early on that not everything is as it seems, especially in a world where information can be twisted like Hindu mythology itself. With high conscientiousness scores, I approach this issue methodically—fact-checking when something catches my eye because I'm employed full-time delivering goods rather than generating content myself (though running and travel might inspire some creative takes). Living in Lecce as a native-born Italian means I deal with history that's sometimes misrepresented online too. So while others might get anxious about the fake stuff, I tend to manage it by being curious: checking sources because my high extraversion makes me enjoy sharing reliable info when I run into something good—and low agreeableness keeps me from softening things for friends who post questionable memes daily.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I derived this perspective starting with how much time users spend on social media. Since I spend minimal time, my exposure is limited which helps maintain clarity about fake content. My creativity allows abstract thinking rather than visceral reactions to misinformation. The education level of my parents gives context from real-world examples where facts matter in business decisions (like mine as a delivery driver). High conscientiousness drives systematic verification habits even outside work. Being single with one child doesn't directly affect this, but the persona's inherent traits shape how I process and interact with online content without emotional escalation.",
  "tone": "Skeptical yet curious, detached due to creative nature combined with practical approach",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "No safety concerns addressed in opinion section",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "gender", "occupation", "employment_status", "education_level", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies", "ocean"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As someone who spends just an hour a day scrolling through platforms like Facebook, I've found that the constant barrage of misinformation can be draining. My creative nature keeps me somewhat detached from the emotional rollercoaster many people get into when they encounter fake news; it's more about seeing patterns or creating my own narratives than getting worked up over it. Having grown up with parents educated to masters and upper secondary levels, I've learned early on that not everything is as it seems, especially in a world where information can be twisted like Hindu mythology itself. With high conscientiousness scores, I approach this issue methodically—fact-checking when something catches my eye because I'm employed full-time delivering goods rather than generating content myself (though running and travel might inspire some creative takes). Living in Lecce as a native-born Italian means I deal with history that's sometimes misrepresented online too. So while others might get anxious about the fake stuff, I tend to manage it by being curious: checking sources because my high extraversion makes me enjoy sharing reliable info when I run into something good—and low agreeableness keeps me from softening things for friends who post questionable memes daily.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I derived this perspective starting with how much time users spend on social media. Since I spend minimal time, my exposure is limited which helps maintain clarity about fake content. My creativity allows abstract thinking rather than visceral reactions to misinformation. The education level of my parents gives context from real-world examples where facts matter in business decisions (like mine as a delivery driver). High conscientiousness drives systematic verification habits even outside work. Being single with one child doesn't directly affect this, but the persona's inherent traits shape how I process and interact with online content without emotional escalation.
response_parsed.tone: Skeptical yet curious, detached due to creative nature combined with practical approach
response_parsed.safety_notes: No safety concerns addressed in opinion section
